topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights seemed to catch the canvas in an entirely new, alien way. I shuffled closer, my breath catching in my throat as I took in the scale of the piece—my large, sprawling landscape, 'Ephemeral Dawn,' now dominating the main wall. It wasn't just displayed; it was curated, framed by stark white pedestals, surrounded by hushed, reverent murmurs from the patrons. My hands instinctively clenched into fists at my sides, the air suddenly feeling too thin to draw into my lungs. I scanned the room frantically, searching for the familiar, slightly askew easel that usually anchored my studio corner. When I finally spotted it, tucked away in a back room, I felt a strange, cold hollowness settle in my chest. I walked toward the artwork, my steps slow and deliberate, my eyes tracing the brushstrokes I had poured my very soul into, now existing in this public space, entirely divorced from my will.

[story 2]

"Is that...?" The sound of my own voice was a dry rasp. I stood just outside the velvet rope, my knuckles white where I gripped the railing. The painting—my portrait series, the one I’d spent three years perfecting—was hanging directly above the entrance. People were stopping, pointing, their faces tilted upward, absorbing the intensity of the oils and pigments. A wave of profound disorientation washed over me. I blinked rapidly, trying to process the scene: the way the light fractured across the impasto, the way strangers were leaning in, their expressions rapt. I took an involuntary step back, bumping against a nearby pillar. My jaw tightened; I needed to move, to reclaim my space, but my feet felt rooted to the spot, paralyzed by the sheer audacity of the display.

[story 3]

I found the notification first, a terse email detailing the installation schedule. Then I saw it—a photograph of my work, perfectly lit, hanging in a space I hadn't booked, a space I hadn't agreed to. A sudden, sharp intake of breath escaped me. My gaze darted from the screen to the actual location, a sterile, brightly lit hall where my abstract sculptures were positioned like museum artifacts. A tremor ran through my hands, making the phone slip slightly in my grasp. I stood frozen by the doorway, the sound of polite applause echoing faintly from within. My mouth opened, but no sound emerged. The reality of the situation—my creation, on display without my consent—felt like a physical weight pressing down on my shoulders, making it difficult to draw a full, steady breath.
